questions.question:
If the list of letters P, R, S, T, U is an arithmetic sequence, which of the following are also in arithmetic sequence?
I.  2P, 2R, 2S, 2T, 2U
II.  P-3, R-3, S-3, T-3, U-3
III. P^2, R^2, S^2, T^2, U^2

A. I only
B. I and II
C. II and III
D. I and III
questions.to_find: which of the three derived sequences are also arithmetic progressions

understand.plain: A sequence is arithmetic if the difference between consecutive terms is constant. If P, R, S, T, U form an AP with common difference d, we must check which transformations preserve this constant-difference property. Multiplying every term by the same constant preserves the ratio of differences, so 2P, 2R, ... is still an AP. Subtracting the same constant from every term shifts all terms equally, leaving the differences unchanged, so P-3, R-3, ... is also an AP. Squaring, however, changes the differences: the difference between consecutive squares depends on the actual values, not just d, so P^2, R^2, ... is generally NOT an AP.

keywords.explain: a sequence where consecutive terms differ by a fixed constant d (the common difference)
the constant added to each term to get the next; d = a_(n+1) - a_n
keywords.example: if P = 1, R = 3, S = 5, T = 7, U = 9 then d = 2

keywords.explain: multiplying or shifting each term by a constant keeps differences constant, hence preserves the AP property
keywords.example: if d = 2 then 2P, 2R have difference 4 — still constant

understand.visual_alt: no figure needed; the question is purely algebraic

given.aim: determine which transformed sequences remain arithmetic progressions

terms.term: P, R, S, T, U in AP
terms.meaning: consecutive terms R-P = S-R = T-S = U-T = d for some common difference d
terms.connects: this is the base sequence from which the three candidates are derived

terms.meaning: each term is multiplied by 2; new common difference = 2d
terms.connects: multiplying all terms by a constant scales d but keeps AP structure

terms.term: Sequence II: P-3, R-3, S-3, T-3, U-3
terms.meaning: each term is decreased by 3; new common difference is still d (unchanged)
terms.connects: adding or subtracting a constant shifts all terms equally, preserving d

terms.term: Sequence III: P^2, R^2, S^2, T^2, U^2
terms.meaning: squaring changes differences: R^2 - P^2 = (R+P)d which varies as terms grow
terms.connects: non-linear transformation breaks the constant-difference property

given.plan: check whether the common difference is constant for each transformed sequence by expressing the differences in terms of d and the original terms

steps.title: Define the AP and check Sequence I
steps.apply: For I: (2R) - (2P) = 2(R-P) = 2d; (2S) - (2R) = 2d; ... all differences equal 2d. Sequence I is an AP.
steps.note: Scalar multiplication preserves the AP property.

steps.title: Check Sequence II
steps.apply: For II: (R-3) - (P-3) = R-P = d; all differences equal d. Sequence II is an AP.
steps.note: Adding or subtracting a constant from every term does not change the common difference.

steps.title: Check Sequence III
steps.apply: For III: the difference R^2 - P^2 = (R+P)d and S^2 - R^2 = (S+R)d. Since R+P != S+R in general, the differences vary. Sequence III is NOT an AP (counterexample: P=1,R=4,S=7 gives 16-1=15, 49-16=33, not equal).
steps.note: Squaring introduces a factor that depends on the actual value, breaking the constant-difference rule.

solution.result: Only Sequences I and II are arithmetic progressions. Answer: B. I and II.

questions.formula_note: No standard registry formula is needed; the AP property is verified directly from the definition of constant differences.

questions.question:
The difference between the sum of the first 2n natural numbers and the sum of the first n odd natural numbers is ______.

A. n^2 = n
B. n^2 + n
C. 2n^2 = n
D. 2n^2 + n
questions.to_find: the difference between the sum of the first 2n natural numbers and the sum of the first n odd natural numbers

understand.plain: The sum of the first 2n natural numbers is the triangular-number formula applied to 2n. The first n odd natural numbers are 1, 3, 5, ..., (2n-1); their sum equals n^2. Subtracting the second from the first gives the answer.

keywords.term: sum of first n odd numbers

understand.visual_alt: no figure needed; pure algebraic manipulation

given.aim: compute S(2n) - S_odd(n) as a closed-form expression in n

terms.term: S(2n) = sum of first 2n natural numbers
terms.connects: this is the minuend in the subtraction

terms.term: S_odd(n) = sum of first n odd natural numbers
terms.connects: this is the subtrahend in the subtraction
this is the quantity we need to express in simplified form

given.plan: apply the triangular-number formula and the odd-sum identity, then subtract and simplify

steps.title: Sum of first 2n natural numbers
steps.note: Substitute m = 2n into the standard triangular-number formula.

steps.title: Sum of first n odd natural numbers
steps.apply: S_odd(n) = n^2. (These n terms form an AP with first term 1, last term 2n-1, so sum = n*(1 + (2n-1))/2 = n^2.)
steps.note: A well-known result: the sum of the first n odd numbers is always a perfect square.

steps.title: Compute the difference
steps.note: Verify with n=3: S(6) = 21, S_odd(3) = 9, difference = 12 = 9+3 = n^2+n. Correct.

solution.result: The difference is n^2 + n. Answer: B. n^2 + n.

questions.formula_note: The sum of first 2n naturals uses the standard triangular-number formula; the sum of first n odds uses the identity sum = n^2.

questions.question:
Insert seven numbers between 2 and 34, such that the resulting sequence including 2 and 34 is an arithmetic progression. The sum of these inserted seven numbers is ______.

A. 120
B. 126
C. 126
D. 130
questions.to_find: the sum of the seven numbers inserted between 2 and 34 to form an AP

understand.plain: When 7 numbers are inserted between 2 and 34, the full sequence has 9 terms (2 at each end plus 7 in between). This 9-term AP has first term 2 and last term 34. The sum of all 9 terms can be computed from the AP sum formula. Subtracting the first and last terms (2 and 34) gives the sum of the 7 inserted terms.

keywords.explain: S_n = n/2 * (first term + last term) when both endpoints are known
d = (last - first) / (n-1) where n is the total number of terms

understand.visual_alt: no figure needed; the question is about an arithmetic sequence of numbers

given.aim: find the sum of the 7 inserted terms in the AP from 2 to 34

terms.term: first term a = 2, last term l = 34
terms.meaning: the two boundary values of the extended AP
terms.example: a=2 and l=34 are fixed; 7 numbers go between them
terms.connects: these anchor the AP so the total number of terms is 9

terms.example: sequence: 2, a2, a3, a4, a5, a6, a7, a8, 34
terms.connects: n determines d via d = (l - a)/(n-1)
subtract endpoints to isolate the 7 inserted values

given.plan: compute the total AP sum for 9 terms, then subtract the two known endpoint values to get the sum of the 7 inserted numbers

steps.title: Determine total number of terms and common difference
Sum of all 9 terms
steps.note: Use the endpoint form of the AP sum since both extremes are known.

steps.title: Sum of inserted seven numbers
steps.note: Alternatively, the 7 inserted numbers also form an AP whose middle term is the average of 2 and 34, i.e. 18; their sum = 7 * 18 = 126.

solution.result: The sum of the seven inserted numbers is 126. Answer: C. 126.

questions.formula_note: The AP endpoint-sum formula S_n = n/2*(a+l) is used; the inserted-sum shortcut (7 * middle term) confirms the result.

questions.question:
In a locality, the houses are numbered in the following way: The house numbers on one side of a road are consecutive odd integers starting from 301, while the house numbers on the other side of the road are consecutive even numbers starting from 302. The total number of houses is the same on both sides of the road.

If the difference of the sum of house numbers between the two sides of the road is 27, then the number of houses on each side of the road is:

A. 27
B. 52
C. 54
D. 26
questions.to_find: number of houses on each side of the road

understand.plain: One side has n consecutive odd numbers starting at 301 (301, 303, 305, ...) and the other side has n consecutive even numbers starting at 302 (302, 304, 306, ...). Each odd house at position k has number 301 + 2(k-1) and the matching even house has number 302 + 2(k-1). The difference for each pair is exactly 1. Summing over all n pairs gives a total difference of n.

keywords.explain: odd numbers spaced 2 apart; the k-th term starting from 301 is 301 + 2(k-1)
even numbers spaced 2 apart; the k-th term starting from 302 is 302 + 2(k-1)
S_even - S_odd = sum of individual differences; each pair (even_k - odd_k) = 1
keywords.example: 302-301 = 1, 304-303 = 1, so n pairs give difference n

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 480 180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><rect x='10' y='20' width='460' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Even side: 302, 304, 306, ...</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='460' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='142' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Odd side: 301, 303, 305, ...</text><text x='240' y='96' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>diff per pair = 1  |  n pairs  =>  total diff = n</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: two rows of house numbers: even side above (302, 304, 306) and odd side below (301, 303, 305), each pair differing by 1

given.aim: find n (number of houses per side) such that S_even - S_odd = 27

terms.meaning: sum of n terms of AP with a=301, d=2: S_odd = n/2 * (2*301 + (n-1)*2) = n(300 + n) = 300n + n^2
terms.connects: this is subtracted from S_even to find the difference

terms.meaning: sum of n terms of AP with a=302, d=2: S_even = n/2 * (2*302 + (n-1)*2) = n(301 + n) = 301n + n^2

terms.connects: setting this equal to 27 directly gives n

given.plan: write AP sum formulas for both sides, subtract, and solve the resulting simple equation

steps.title: Sum of n odd-side house numbers
steps.note: AP with first term 301 and common difference 2.

steps.title: Sum of n even-side house numbers
steps.note: AP with first term 302 and common difference 2.

steps.title: Set difference equal to 27 and solve
steps.apply: S_even - S_odd = (301n + n^2) - (300n + n^2) = n. Setting n = 27.
steps.note: The n^2 terms cancel and n^2 terms cancel completely, leaving simply n = 27.

solution.result: The number of houses on each side is 27. Answer: A. 27.

questions.formula_note: AP sum formula S_n = n/2*(2a + (n-1)d) is applied to both sides; the n^2 terms cancel leaving a linear equation.

questions.question:
If the sum of the first 20 consecutive positive odd numbers is divided by 20^2, the result is:

A. 1
B. 20
C. 2
D. 1/2
questions.to_find: the value of (sum of first 20 consecutive positive odd numbers) divided by 20^2

understand.plain: The first n consecutive positive odd numbers are 1, 3, 5, ..., (2n-1). Their sum equals n^2. Here n = 20, so the sum is 20^2 = 400. Dividing by 20^2 = 400 gives exactly 1.

keywords.term: sum of first n odd numbers
keywords.explain: 1 + 3 + 5 + ... + (2n-1) = n^2; a classical result provable by induction or AP formula
if the sum equals n^2, dividing by n^2 gives 1 regardless of n

understand.visual_alt: no figure needed; purely algebraic identity

given.aim: compute (sum of first 20 positive odd numbers) / 20^2

terms.example: the 5th odd number is 9; the 20th is 39
terms.connects: these are the terms to be summed

terms.term: sum of first n odd numbers = n^2
terms.meaning: proven by AP formula: sum = n/2*(1 + (2n-1)) = n/2 * 2n = n^2
terms.connects: this gives the numerator of the expression

terms.meaning: the denominator equals n^2 when n=20
terms.connects: numerator and denominator are equal, so the result is 1

given.plan: apply the identity sum of first n odds = n^2 with n=20, then divide by 20^2

steps.title: Sum of first 20 positive odd numbers
Divide by 20^2
steps.note: In general, sum of first n odd numbers divided by n^2 always equals 1.

questions.formula_note: The key identity is that the sum of the first n positive odd integers equals n^2; dividing by n^2 gives 1.

questions.question:
The sum of the first n terms in the sequence 5, 55, 555, 5555, ... is:

A. (5/81)(10^(n+1) - 9n - 10)
B. (5/81)(10^(n+1) - 9n - 10)
C. (5/81)(10^(n+1) + 9n - 10)
D. (5/81)(10^(n+1) - 9n + 10)
questions.to_find: the sum of the first n terms of the series 5, 55, 555, 5555, ...

understand.plain: Each term in the series 5, 55, 555, ... can be written as 5/9 times (10^1 - 1), 5/9 times (10^2 - 1), ..., so the k-th term is (5/9)(10^k - 1). The sum S_n is (5/9) times [sum of 10^k for k=1 to n minus n]. The geometric sum sum_{k=1}^{n} 10^k = 10(10^n - 1)/9. Combining these gives the closed form.

keywords.explain: each term is 5 repeated k times = 5*(10^k - 1)/9
combining the geometric and arithmetic parts yields (5/81)(10^(n+1) - 9n - 10)
keywords.example: n=1: (5/81)(100 - 9 - 10) = (5/81)*81 = 5. Correct, since the first term is 5.

understand.visual_alt: no figure needed; the question involves pure algebraic manipulation of a series

given.aim: derive S_n = sum of the first n terms of 5, 55, 555, ...

terms.connects: expressing T_k this way allows separation into a geometric and a constant part
split the sum into a geometric series and n constant terms
this is the main non-trivial sum needed in the calculation

given.plan: write each term as (5/9)(10^k - 1), factor out (5/9), sum the geometric part and the constant part separately, and simplify

steps.title: Express the k-th term
steps.note: Every term is a repunit (all-fives number) scaled to fit this formula.

steps.title: Write the sum and factor out (5/9)
steps.note: Factor (5/9) applies to both the geometric sum and the n constant terms.

steps.title: Evaluate the geometric sum
steps.note: Geometric series with first term 10, ratio 10, n terms.

steps.title: Combine and simplify
steps.note: Verify n=1: (5/81)*(100 - 9 - 10) = (5/81)*81 = 5. First term is 5. Correct.

questions.formula_note: The geometric series formula for sum_{k=1}^{n} 10^k is the key step; the rest is algebraic simplification.
